title: Robbie's Statue
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grown tired of mourning for my sins, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And brooding over merits, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The other night with bothered brow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I went amongst the spirits;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I met one that I knew well:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh, Scottyן¿½s Ghost, is that you?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And did you see the fearsome crowd
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½At Robbie Burnsן¿½s statue?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They hurried up in hansom cabs,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tall-hatted and frock-coated;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They trained it in from all the towns,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The weird and hairy-throated;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They spoke in some outlandish tongue,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They cut some comic capers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And ilka man was wild to get
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½His name in all the papers.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They showed no gleam of intellect,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Those frauds who rushed before us;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They knew one verse of ן¿½Auld Lang Syne, ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The first one and the chorus:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They clacked the clack oן¿½ Scotlanן¿½s Bard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They glibly talked of ן¿½Rabby;ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But what if he had come to them
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Without a groat and shabby?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They drank and wept for Robbieן¿½s sake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They stood and brayed like asses
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½(The living bardן¿½s a drunken rake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The dead one loved the lasses);
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½If Robbie Burns were here, theyן¿½d sit
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As still as any mouse is;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½If Robbie Burns should come their way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Theyן¿½d turn him out their houses.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh, weep for bonny Scotlandן¿½s bard!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And praise the Scottish nation,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Who made him spy and let him die
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Heart-broken in privation:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Exciseman, so that he might live
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Through northern wintersן¿½ rigours, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Just as in southern lands they give
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The hard-up rhymer figures.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We need some songs of stinging fun
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To wake the States and light ן¿½em;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I wish a man like Robert Burns
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Were here to-day to write ן¿½em!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But still the mockery shall survive
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Till the Day oן¿½ Judgment crashes, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The men we scorn when weן¿½re alive
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With praise insult our ashes.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Scottyן¿½s ghost said: ן¿½Never mind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The fleas that you inherit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The living bard can flick them off, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They cannot hurt his spirit.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The crawlers round the bardieן¿½s name
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Shall crawl through all the ages;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½His workן¿½s the living thing, and they
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Are fly-dirt on the pages.ן¿½
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
